Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Bars toggle between B (active) and w (inactive). Shape 2 moves LEFT when row 1 bars are active, but movement is rejected (shape returns). Bars didn't fully reset after blue circle - only the toggled bar changed. Need to understand bar-to-movement mapping.
- Plan: Click blue circle to see if it resets bars, then test individual bars systematically.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 133 actions; a strong score needs about 72 or fewer. You are 1.8x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The bars are still in the same state: row 1 cols 2-4 are B, everything else is w. The blue circle click didn't reset the bars further. So I need to manually reset them.
Let me reset the remaining B bars (33, 44), (33, 49), (33, 54) by clicking them, then test with a single bar.
But wait, I've been spending too many actions on probing. Let me think about what I know:
1. Shape 2 moves LEFT when row 1 bars are active.
2. The movement is rejected (shape returns to original position).
3. The left panel shows a "C" shape at rows 16-19, cols 6-9.
4. The right panel has shapes at rows 8 and 25, cols 46.
Maybe the goal is to move the shapes to match the left panel target position. But the shapes only move horizontally, not vertically. So maybe the bar rows determine which shape moves, and the bar columns determine the direction.
Let me think about the bar row mapping:
- 6 bar rows, 2 shapes
- Maybe rows 1-3 control shape 1, and rows 4-6 control shape 2?
- Or maybe each row controls a different movement amount?
Actually, let me reconsider the game entirely. Looking at the left panel:
- The "C" shape is at rows 16-19, cols 6-9.
- The bars at the bottom have row 1 all active (B B B B).
Looking at the right panel:
- Two yellow shapes at rows 8 and 25, cols 46.
- The bars at the bottom are currently w B B B for row 1.
Maybe the left panel bars represent the "target" movement pattern, and I need to set the right panel bars to match. Currently, the left panel has row 1 all active. If I set the right panel row 1 to all active, the shapes should move to match the target.
But when I tried this before, the shapes moved left and then returned. Maybe the movement was correct but the position was wrong because the shapes need to move in a different direction.
Wait, let me look at the left panel shape position more carefully. The "C" shape is at rows 16-19, cols 6-9. This is in the LEFT part of the left panel display area. Maybe this indicates that the target position is on the LEFT side.
The right panel shapes are at col 46, which is in the middle of the right panel display area (cols 34-63). If the target is on the left, the shapes need to move LEFT.
But when I moved the shapes left, they went off screen and returned. Maybe the movement amount was too large.
Let me try moving the shapes left by a smaller amount. If the bar columns represent movement amounts:
- Col 1: move 1 unit
- Col 2: move 2 units
- Col 3: move 4 units
- Col 4: move 8 units
Then setting col 1 only would move the shape by 1 unit left.
But I don't know if this is the correct mapping. Let me try setting only col 1 in row 1 and see what happens.
First, I need to reset the current B bars. Let me click (33, 44), (33, 49), (33, 54) to toggle them to w, then set (33, 39) to B, and click the blue circle.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Bars still have row 1 cols 2-4 as B. Blue circle doesn't auto-reset bars. Need to manually reset and test individual bars. Shape 2 moves left when row 1 bars active, but movement rejected.
Plan: Reset row 1 cols 2-4, set only col 1, test movement. This probes whether individual columns control different movement amounts.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Reset row 1 cols 2-4 (toggle B to w), then set col 1, then click blue circle
action([
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 44},  # toggle to w
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 49},  # toggle to w
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 54},  # toggle to w
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 33, 'col': 39},  # toggle to B (was w)
    {'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 55, 'col': 46}   # blue circle
])

# Check animation
if last_animation:
    for i, entry in enumerate(last_animation):
        if len(entry.frames) > 1:
            print(f"Action {i} ({entry.action}): {len(entry.frames)} frames")
            for j, frame in enumerate(entry.frames):
                seg = frame.segmentation
                y_shapes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'Y' and n['pixels'] > 10]
                if y_shapes:
                    centers = [(round(sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), 1), 
                               round(sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']), 1)) for n in y_shapes]
                    print(f"  Frame {j}: {centers}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
